meta.title: A COVID Journal: An Infectious Diseases Pharmacist's Pandemic Chronicle
meta.author: Steven Smoke
meta.description: Structured chapter data extracted from the Covid-19 Timeline document for use in a React-based interactive website.

chapters.title: The Before Times
chapters.subtitle: From First Reports to First Cases
chapters.mood_description: A calm before the storm. Casual dismissal of early warning signs, life proceeding normally — Rutgers basketball, work trips, grocery runs.
chapters.introduction: I was on my honeymoon when the first cases were reported in Wuhan. I didn't think much of it. None of us did. There had been outbreaks before — SARS, MERS, Ebola, Zika — and they had never really reached us. This chapter is about the period before we understood what was coming, and how easy it was to look the other way.

clinical_nodes.label: First Cases
clinical_nodes.content: First reported cases of an unknown respiratory infection in Wuhan, China.

clinical_nodes.label: WHO Bulletin
clinical_nodes.content: Novel coronavirus (2019-nCoV) outbreak noted on Twitter / WHO.
clinical_nodes.preview: Novel coronavirus (2019-nCoV) outbreak noted on Twitter / WHO.

sections.heading: The First I Heard of It
sections.text: We had just returned from our honeymoon in Austria. I had been completely off the radar — no clinical news, no alerts. This WHO bulletin was genuinely the first time the outbreak registered for me at all. It didn't set off any alarms. There had been outbreaks before — SARS, MERS, Ebola, Zika — and I was familiar enough with each of them to know they had never really reached us. A novel coronavirus from Wuhan felt like a variation on a familiar pattern.

sections.heading: The Conversation That Felt Obvious
sections.text: I remember speaking with colleagues in the pharmacy department about it around this time — going through the prior outbreaks, what they were, how they spread, why they hadn't become a domestic crisis. It felt like a reasonable and informed way to contextualize what we were seeing. In retrospect, it's a little embarrassing. None of those outbreaks had come close to our country, let alone our hospital. I was using the right historical examples to draw exactly the wrong conclusion.

clinical_nodes.label: First US Case
clinical_nodes.content: First confirmed US case reported in Washington State.

clinical_nodes.label: System Infectious Diseases Meeting
clinical_nodes.content: Novel coronavirus (nCoV-2019) officially renamed Covid-19. Topics: employee travel screening, mask shortage, testing protocols.
clinical_nodes.preview: Novel coronavirus (nCoV-2019) officially renamed Covid-19. Topics: employee travel screening, mask shortage, testing protocols.

sections.heading: Distant, Hypothetical, Foreign
sections.text: There was a buzz about Covid in this meeting — it was on the agenda, it was being discussed seriously. But the feeling in the room didn't match the moment. It still felt distant. Hypothetical. Something happening elsewhere, to other people, in a system very different from ours. The topics were real — travel screening, mask supply, testing — but the urgency beneath them wasn't. In retrospect, that urgency was exactly what was needed, and it wasn't there.

clinical_nodes.label: Italy: The West's First Major Outbreak
clinical_nodes.content: Italy reports a rapidly escalating outbreak. Overwhelmed hospitals, impossible triage decisions. A turning point in understanding what was coming.
clinical_nodes.preview: Italy reports a rapidly escalating outbreak. Overwhelmed hospitals, impossible triage decisions. A turning point in understanding what was coming.

sections.heading: A New Region, A New Reality
sections.text: There had been reports from China — alarming ones — but they came with a significant asterisk: we couldn't be sure how much to trust them. Uncertainty about the data made it easier to rationalize. Italy was different. This was a western country with transparent reporting infrastructure, and the images coming out were unlike anything most of us had ever seen. Overwhelmed ICUs. Doctors making impossible triage decisions in hallways. Seeing the outbreak spread to a new region felt like a significant escalation — the kind that was harder to dismiss.

clinical_nodes.label: Puerto Rico Work Trip
clinical_nodes.content: Extended discussion with a Population Health physician during a flight delay about openness to travel amid the impending Covid-19 wave. He poses a hypothetical: would I travel in May?
clinical_nodes.preview: Extended discussion with a Population Health physician during a flight delay about openness to travel amid the impending Covid-19 wave. He poses a hypothetical: would I travel in May?

sections.heading: A Reasonable Question, A Detached Answer
sections.text: The question wasn't absurd at all — it was exactly the kind of thing a thoughtful person should have been asking in early March 2020. What was absurd was my answer. I was so oblivious to what was actually coming that whatever I said had almost no connection to reality. Italy was on television. The trajectory was visible if you were willing to follow it. I wasn't. I was still reasoning as if this was a thing happening elsewhere, to other people, that would probably stay that way.

clinical_nodes.label: First NJ Case
clinical_nodes.content: First confirmed New Jersey case reported in Fort Lee.

personal_nodes.label: Austria Honeymoon
personal_nodes.content: My wife and I travel to Austria for our honeymoon — Vienna, Salzburg, and Zell am See.

personal_nodes.label: AMS Prep Meeting
personal_nodes.content: During preparation for an Antimicrobial Stewardship (AMS) meeting, an ID physician colleague mentions that coronavirus might be a big deal. I dismiss it — I wanted to stay focused on things already impacting our patients, and it seemed unlikely this would ever reach us. I could not have been more wrong.

personal_nodes.label: Dismissal
personal_nodes.content: I see the WHO coronavirus bulletin on Twitter and don't think much of it — mentally file it with prior outbreaks (Zika, Ebola, SARS, MERS) that never really spread substantially.

personal_nodes.label: Rutgers Basketball
personal_nodes.content: Rutgers beats #21 Illinois. Program on track for first NCAA tournament berth in 30 years.
personal_nodes.preview: Rutgers beats #21 Illinois. Program on track for first NCAA tournament berth in 30 years.

sections.heading: A Lifetime of Letdowns
sections.text: Never in my lifetime had Rutgers men's basketball made the NCAA tournament. To be a Rutgers fan was to be fluent in disappointment — close calls that weren't close, early exits, and seasons that faded quietly into nothing. And yet, here we were. The energy around the program was electric. Something felt genuinely different.

personal_nodes.label: My Wife Prepares
personal_nodes.content: While I'm in Puerto Rico, my wife begins preparing for a potential Covid-19 wave — first grocery haul and cleaning supplies.

chapters.title: The Run Up
chapters.subtitle: March 9–13, 2020: The Week Everything Changed
chapters.mood_description: Five days of cascading cancellations, first treatment emails, and the dawning realization that this is real. The world of sports collapses in real time.
chapters.introduction: Five days. That's all it took for the world to come apart. The NBA suspended its season. March Madness was canceled. A colleague told us we were moving too slowly, and she was right. By Friday, I was drafting treatment protocols for a disease we barely understood. Nothing about the pace of that week has ever felt normal in retrospect.

clinical_nodes.label: First Remdesivir Email
clinical_nodes.content: The Chief Pharmacy Officer sends the first corporate email discussing remdesivir. Position: do not advocate without clinical trial data, but IND compassionate use is reasonable for highest-risk, critically ill patients.

clinical_nodes.label: System Infectious Diseases Meeting — A Warning Issued
clinical_nodes.content: A chronically irritable ID physician berates the group: the system is moving too slowly. 'We need to prepare for what transmission will be, not what it currently is. We will be overrun.' The next 3 meetings are canceled.
clinical_nodes.preview: A chronically irritable ID physician berates the group: the system is moving too slowly. The next 3 meetings are canceled.

sections.heading: She Was Right
sections.text: I remember her message hitting hard — harder than most things I'd heard in a meeting room. She was blunt, visibly frustrated, and not particularly diplomatic about it. At the time, some of that got in the way of the message. But when the world turned upside down just days later, I remembered exactly what she had said. She had seen it coming. She had told us. And we hadn't moved fast enough.

clinical_nodes.label: First ID Pharmacy Collaborative Email
clinical_nodes.content: I send the inaugural email to the ID Pharmacy Collaborative sharing a treatment Google doc. I anticipate 'constantly changing evidence.'
clinical_nodes.preview: I send the inaugural email to the ID Pharmacy Collaborative sharing a treatment Google doc. I anticipate 'constantly changing evidence.'

sections.heading: Where It Came From
sections.text: The document I shared wasn't mine. I had found it on Twitter — a pharmacist at UCSF had started a Google Doc and shared it openly, globally, for anyone to contribute to or use. That was a genuinely novel approach at the time. The idea that you would crowdsource clinical guidance across institutions in real time, across the country, without waiting for formal channels — it reflected exactly the kind of collaboration the moment required. I was right to share it with our team. But I want to be clear about what I was actually doing: I was recognizing and mirroring a brilliant and insightful idea that originated on the other side of the country.

sections.heading: Initial Assessments — March 11, 2020
sections.text: Tamiflu unlikely to work. Remdesivir under NIH study. Chloroquine shows 'promising but questionable' evidence from China. Lopinavir/ritonavir retrospective shows no efficacy.

clinical_nodes.label: Call for Standard Algorithm
clinical_nodes.content: I email the AMS Chair, the Chief Pharmacy Officer, and Corporate Clinical Pharmacy leadership: 'We need a standard treatment algorithm.'

clinical_nodes.label: First Draft Treatment Protocol
clinical_nodes.content: Our ID PharmD colleague sends a draft treatment protocol — revised 3-4 times in one day.
clinical_nodes.preview: Our ID PharmD colleague sends a draft treatment protocol — revised 3-4 times in one day.

sections.heading: Protocol Framework
sections.text: Two pathways: one for ID-managed patients, one for surge overflow. Criteria: ICU / supplemental O2 / high-risk. Preferred: remdesivir compassionate use, then hydroxychloroquine. If ICU: sarilumab via trial or tocilizumab.

sections.heading: Pretty Damn Good, In Retrospect
sections.text: Looking back at this protocol with the benefit of everything we learned over the next two years — it holds up. Not perfectly, and not every choice aged well. But given that it was written in a single day, revised three or four times before it was even finalized, with almost no reliable evidence to draw from and a disease nobody fully understood yet, it was a remarkably sound starting point. The structure was right. The instincts were right. That's worth acknowledging.

clinical_nodes.label: Pediatric ID Physician Remdesivir Inquiry
clinical_nodes.content: The Pediatric ID physician asks about the process to obtain remdesivir for pediatric use.
clinical_nodes.preview: The Pediatric ID physician asks about the process to obtain remdesivir for pediatric use.

sections.heading: A Process I'd Come to Know Well
sections.text: This was my first real foray into compassionate use — navigating the request process, understanding what was required, figuring out who to contact. It was unfamiliar territory in March 2020. Over the next few months, it would become anything but. Remdesivir, sarilumab, casirivimab/imdevimab — compassionate use became a recurring part of the job, and this inquiry was where that started.

personal_nodes.label: Distracted by Covid
personal_nodes.content: I'm supposed to be prepping for an ID society meeting presentation but can't focus — convinced this will be a major problem after seeing community transmission data from Washington State.

personal_nodes.label: NBA Positive Test
personal_nodes.content: An NBA player tests positive; the NBA season is suspended.
personal_nodes.preview: An NBA player tests positive; the NBA season is suspended.

sections.heading: The First Domino
sections.text: This was the moment the pandemic stopped being a news story and became something else. A positive test, a season suspended mid-game — it landed differently than hospital data or case counts or footage from overseas. Sports are woven into the fabric of how people mark time and gather and feel normal. When the NBA stopped, something at the pop culture level cracked. It was the first domino, and everyone felt it. Whatever had been hypothetical or distant was no longer either of those things.

personal_nodes.label: Big Ten Tournament Canceled
personal_nodes.content: The B10 tournament is canceled while Rutgers is on the court warming up — the tournament dream dies. The NHL season is also suspended.
personal_nodes.preview: The B10 tournament is canceled while Rutgers is on the court warming up — the tournament dream dies. The NHL season is also suspended.

sections.heading: Classically Rutgers
sections.text: Of all the ways a once-in-a-generation season could end, this had to be it. A once-in-a-hundred-year global pandemic — arriving at the exact moment Rutgers basketball was finally, undeniably, headed to the NCAA tournament for the first time in thirty years. The cruelty of the timing was almost poetic. It was classically Rutgers: find a way to lose that no one could have ever scripted.

personal_nodes.label: NCAA March Madness Canceled
personal_nodes.content: NCAA March Madness officially canceled.

personal_nodes.label: ID Society Meeting Canceled
personal_nodes.content: The state infectious diseases society cancels its meeting.

image12 — Photo associated with March 12 Big Ten / NHL cancellations

chapters.title: Exponential Growth
chapters.subtitle: The Hydroxychloroquine Wars
chapters.mood_description: A frantic three weeks of evolving guidelines, drug shortages, political interference, Twitter misinformation, and inter-physician conflict over HCQ dosing. The science is moving faster than anyone can track.
chapters.introduction: This was the period where the science couldn't keep up with the crisis, and the politics couldn't stay out of the science. We were writing and rewriting treatment guidelines in real time, debating drugs we had almost no evidence for, and fielding questions no one could answer. Every day brought a new study, a new shortage, a new argument. I described my role at the time as an offensive coordinator — calling plays from the booth, not from the field. That metaphor held up better than most of the treatments we recommended.

clinical_nodes.label: Ibuprofen Controversy
clinical_nodes.content: The French Health Minister tweets that ibuprofen could be an aggravating factor. Anecdotal and pharmacological speculation, not evidence-based. WHO temporarily recommends against ibuprofen; WHO and EMA later retract on 3/19.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Stockpile Revealed
clinical_nodes.content: The Chief Pharmacy Officer shares that our health system has stockpiled hydroxychloroquine and requests an algorithm for distribution.
clinical_nodes.preview: The Chief Pharmacy Officer shares that our health system has stockpiled hydroxychloroquine and requests an algorithm for distribution.

sections.heading: Easier to Call For Than to Create
sections.text: A few days earlier, I had been among those calling for a standard distribution algorithm. This moment was a reminder that calling for one and actually building one are very different things. The request landed back with us — and the work of translating an urgent clinical need into a structured, defensible protocol under time pressure is considerably harder than the email that asked for it.

sections.heading: Mixed Feelings on the Stockpile
sections.text: I had complicated feelings about the stockpile then, and I still do. On one level, it was responsible preparation — protecting our patients in a supply-constrained environment. On another level, it's a zero-sum game. Hydroxychloroquine supply wasn't infinite. Stock we held was stock that wasn't available to patients outside our system, at other hospitals, without the same institutional resources. There's no clean answer to that tension. But I think it's worth sitting with rather than explaining away.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Protocol Version 3
clinical_nodes.content: Our ID PharmD colleague sends updated recommendations: HCQ for most eligible patients, remdesivir via clinical trial if mechanically ventilated, IL-6 antagonist if ARDS. The AMS distributes the first formal guidance (Version 3).

clinical_nodes.label: Steroids Debate Begins
clinical_nodes.content: I share a JAMA retrospective suggesting steroids may help; CDC and WHO recommend against them at this time. Later proven to reduce mortality.
clinical_nodes.preview: I share a JAMA retrospective suggesting steroids may help; CDC and WHO recommend against them at this time. Later proven to reduce mortality.

sections.heading: You Can't See It in the Moment
sections.text: Steroids are one of many examples from this period that illustrate how impossible it is to detect efficacy in real time. The signal was there in observational data, but so was the noise — confounding, selection bias, the absence of a control group. The CDC and WHO said no. The retrospective said maybe. Both were working from incomplete pictures. The only thing that ultimately resolved it was a well-conducted RCT. The RECOVERY trial gave us dexamethasone, definitively, months later. There's no shortcut to that kind of answer. You can be attentive to signals, but you have to be honest about what a signal actually is.

embed.label: Read the Study in JAMA Internal Medicine

clinical_nodes.label: First Staff Pharmacist Presentation
clinical_nodes.content: I give my first Covid-19 presentation to staff pharmacists. Packed room. Most questions cannot be answered.
clinical_nodes.preview: I give my first Covid-19 presentation to staff pharmacists. Packed room. Most questions cannot be answered.

sections.heading: The One Thing I Got Right
sections.text: Standing in front of a packed room of pharmacists, I didn't have many answers. The evidence was thin, the guidelines were shifting, and the honest answer to most questions was 'we don't know yet.' But I did tell them one thing with confidence: whatever we're telling you today is going to change. The virus was new, the data was sparse, and anyone claiming certainty was overreaching. I don't know how convincing it was in the moment, but it was true.

sections.heading: How Effective Was That Message
sections.text: Not as effective as I'd hoped. Even with the warning built in from day one, it was still difficult to redirect staff each time recommendations evolved — and they evolved constantly. People anchor to the first thing they hear. When the guidance shifted, it wasn't always received as 'we said this might happen.' It felt like contradiction. That tension — between communicating uncertainty and being trusted as an authority — was something I'd have to navigate over and over throughout the pandemic.

clinical_nodes.label: NEJM: Lopinavir/r Not Effective
clinical_nodes.content: NEJM RCT published: lopinavir/ritonavir not effective for Covid-19. Removed from future protocol versions.
clinical_nodes.preview: NEJM RCT published: lopinavir/ritonavir not effective for Covid-19. Removed from future protocol versions.

sections.heading: An Extraordinary Feat of Science
sections.text: The result was disappointing — lopinavir/ritonavir was out. But stepping back from the finding itself, what had just happened was remarkable. Researchers in Wuhan had enrolled patients, randomized them, collected outcomes, analyzed the data, written the paper, and gotten it through peer review and into the NEJM in the middle of an active outbreak. The first case in Wuhan had been reported roughly two months earlier. That kind of turnaround, under those conditions, was almost without precedent.

sections.heading: A Negative Result Is Still a Result
sections.text: Lopinavir/ritonavir came off the protocol. That's the right outcome — clinicians needed to know it didn't work so they could stop using it. A null result published rigorously is infinitely more valuable than a promising signal that never gets tested. The trial answered the question. That's what trials are for, and the fact that this one was stood up and executed in the middle of a global emergency made it all the more impressive — even if the answer wasn't the one anyone was hoping for.

clinical_nodes.label: French HCQ Study Circulates
clinical_nodes.content: An ID physician shares the French HCQ/azithromycin study — not yet published or peer-reviewed. Debate begins on dosing, azithromycin addition, and QTc monitoring. Azithromycin goes on ASHP shortage.
clinical_nodes.preview: An ID physician shares the French HCQ/azithromycin study — not yet published or peer-reviewed. Debate begins on dosing, azithromycin addition, and QTc monitoring. Azithromycin goes on ASHP shortage.

sections.heading: This Pushed Us Over the Cliff
sections.text: HCQ wasn't a new idea. There was already preclinical rationale, in vitro data suggesting antiviral activity, and prior experience using it for other indications. The field had been circling it. This study didn't introduce the concept — it provided the push. A small, flawed, not-yet-peer-reviewed preprint from Marseille, and suddenly the conversation shifted from 'should we consider it' to 'how do we use it.'

sections.heading: Quality Was Hard to Focus On
sections.text: The methodological problems were real — an open-label design, a tiny sample, patients excluded post-enrollment, no proper control group. They were visible to anyone who looked. But in the moment, the quality was hard to focus on. People were dying and we had nothing. The signal, however weak, felt like something. And that's the thing about a microcosm of evidence in a climate of fear and uncertainty: a small study with a hopeful result doesn't have to be good to be influential. It just has to arrive at the right moment.

sections.heading: Easier to Say Yes Than to Wait
sections.text: This is what I eventually came to understand about that period. When the evidence is thin and the stakes feel existential, the psychological calculus tilts heavily toward action. It is much easier to say 'let's give it a try' than to say 'let's hold off and do nothing.' Doing nothing feels like abandonment. Doing something — even something unproven — feels like fighting. That instinct isn't irrational. But it's also how we ended up with a drug shortage, cardiac monitoring protocols, and months of chasing a signal that wasn't there.

embed.label: Read the Original Preprint PDF (IHU Marseille)

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Use 'Out of Control'
clinical_nodes.content: I note in an email: 'well our use is starting to get out of control.' First daily HCQ tracking reports begin.

clinical_nodes.label: Vitamin C and Zinc: Evidence-Free Request
clinical_nodes.content: A corporate pharmacy director asks about including Vitamin C and zinc comments in the guideline. After clarification, the request is not based on any evidence — just questions that have come up. A common theme at this stage of the pandemic.
clinical_nodes.preview: A corporate pharmacy director asks about including Vitamin C and zinc comments in the guideline. After clarification, the request is not based on any evidence — just questions that have come up. A common theme at this stage of the pandemic.

sections.heading: The Burden of Proof Flipped
sections.text: This request crystallized something that was becoming a pattern. The default logic had quietly inverted. Normally, you need evidence to try something. During this period, you needed evidence not to try it. In the absence of data pointing against something — no known harm, no obvious contraindication — the reasoning was: why not? Questions were coming up, people wanted answers, and 'we don't know' felt inadequate when patients were deteriorating. So the list of things worth considering kept growing.

sections.heading: All Kinds of Things Were Worth Trying
sections.text: Vitamin C. Zinc. Melatonin. Famotidine. Colchicine. Some of these would eventually get studied. Most would go nowhere. But in March of 2020, the bar for inclusion in the conversation was low — not because people were being reckless, but because the framework had shifted. With nothing proven, everything unproven looked roughly equivalent. The French HCQ study had already demonstrated that a single small paper was enough to move a field. That lesson cut in every direction.

clinical_nodes.label: First Compassionate Use Approved
clinical_nodes.content: My first Gilead compassionate use remdesivir request, submitted on behalf of an ICU physician, is approved.
clinical_nodes.preview: My first Gilead compassionate use remdesivir request, submitted on behalf of an ICU physician, is approved.

sections.heading: A New Process I'd Get Very Familiar With
sections.text: This was my introduction to compassionate use — a process I would come to know intimately over the next year. The bureaucracy was real: multiple forms, multiple sign-offs from the patient, the provider, the IRB, the manufacturer, and the FDA. Inefficient by almost any measure. But every time you actually connected with a human being in that chain, it was someone who genuinely wanted to help. It didn't matter what time you called or what day of the week it was. People picked up. People came through. The system was slow, but the people inside it weren't.

clinical_nodes.label: Tocilizumab Allocation Debate
clinical_nodes.content: ID pharmacists debate the criteria for tocilizumab use. Concerns that the criteria are not specific enough — but there is no clear answer for who it should go to.
clinical_nodes.preview: ID pharmacists debate the criteria for tocilizumab use. Concerns that the criteria are not specific enough — but there is no clear answer for who it should go to.

sections.heading: A Recurring Theme
sections.text: Limited supply adds urgency. Allocating an unproven drug when both the evidence and the stock are thin — a problem that repeats itself throughout the pandemic.

clinical_nodes.label: President Tweets About HCQ
clinical_nodes.content: The President tweets that hydroxychloroquine and azithromycin could be 'one of the biggest game changers in the history of medicine,' bringing massive public attention and intensifying pressure to prescribe both drugs.
clinical_nodes.preview: The President tweets that hydroxychloroquine and azithromycin could be 'one of the biggest game changers in the history of medicine,' bringing massive public attention and intensifying pressure to prescribe both drugs.

sections.heading: The Moment It Left the Hospital
sections.text: Up until this point, HCQ had been a clinical conversation — pharmacists, physicians, researchers, and guideline committees debating evidence quality and dosing thresholds. This tweet ended that. Overnight, hydroxychloroquine wasn't a drug being evaluated in a hospital setting. It was a topic at the dinner table, in text message threads, on the news. The pressure to prescribe intensified immediately and came from everywhere at once.

sections.heading: Completely Surreal
sections.text: This was the moment my normal, everyday work became the thing that everyone in my family and friend circle suddenly wanted to talk about and debate. People who had never asked me a clinical question in my life were texting to ask about hydroxychloroquine. It was completely surreal — to have spent years building expertise in infectious disease pharmacotherapy and then watch that expertise become a political football in real time. The work hadn't changed. The world around it had.

clinical_nodes.label: CBMC Pharmacy Updates to ID Begin
clinical_nodes.content: I begin sending regular updates to the ID division: case numbers, HCQ utilization, medication inventory, and literature summaries. Sent three days a week for the next month and a half.
clinical_nodes.preview: I begin sending regular updates to the ID division: case numbers, HCQ utilization, medication inventory, and literature summaries. Sent three days a week for the next month and a half.

sections.heading: What Prompted It
sections.text: This started because an ID physician had reached out to the division expressing a concern that was hard to argue with: it was becoming impossible to care for the sheer number of patients while simultaneously keeping up with the rapidly evolving conversation about how to manage the disease. The literature was moving daily. Guidelines were shifting. New drugs were being discussed, added, and removed. Clinicians who were spending twelve hours at the bedside didn't have the bandwidth to also track all of it.

sections.heading: A Role That Made Sense
sections.text: That's where pharmacy could help. I wasn't at the bedside the same way — I could carve out time to synthesize what was coming out, track what we had in inventory, and put it in a format that was useful without requiring an hour to read. Three updates a week for a month and a half. It was a small thing, but it addressed a real problem: the people most responsible for managing these patients were the ones least able to step back and take stock of what the evidence actually said.

clinical_nodes.label: Remdesivir Trial Site: Ambition Without Infrastructure
clinical_nodes.content: Physicians frequently raise the idea of becoming a remdesivir clinical trial site. In practice, the infrastructure isn't there.
clinical_nodes.preview: Physicians frequently raise the idea of becoming a remdesivir clinical trial site. In practice, the infrastructure isn't there.

sections.heading: Why It Didn't Happen
sections.text: Shortage of primary investigators with trial experience, limited research infrastructure. We do not become a remdesivir site. A handful of trials are approved through IRB, but very few patients are enrolled.

sections.heading: The Most Valuable Thing We Could Have Done
sections.text: In retrospect, this is one of the clearest missed opportunities of the pandemic at our institution. Of all the things a hospital could focus on during a novel infectious disease outbreak — tracking utilization, managing shortages, updating guidelines — the most important work happening at that moment was enrolling patients in trials. That's how we got dexamethasone. That's how we eventually got answers on remdesivir, on anticoagulation, on everything that actually moved the needle. Every patient enrolled in a well-designed trial was a contribution to the evidence base that everyone else would eventually rely on. We were treating patients anyway. We just weren't capturing what we learned.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Reporting: Wildly Wrong Numbers
clinical_nodes.content: A system-level HCQ report shows just 14 patients across the entire system — meanwhile CBMC alone has 37. Daily manual reconciliation of HCQ numbers is required for corporate reporting throughout most of the first wave.
clinical_nodes.preview: A system-level HCQ report shows just 14 patients across the entire system — meanwhile CBMC alone has 37. Daily manual reconciliation of HCQ numbers is required for corporate reporting throughout most of the first wave.

sections.heading: A Data Infrastructure Problem
sections.text: The gap between 14 and 37 wasn't a sign that HCQ was being undercounted everywhere — it was a sign of how data-poor our own system was. The reporting tools couldn't accurately capture what was actually happening at the hospital level. This wasn't necessarily a problem beyond our walls; it was ours. And the consequence was that someone had to manually reconcile the numbers every day just to produce an accurate report. That became part of the routine for most of the first wave.

clinical_nodes.label: ACEi/ARB as Solution: Early Misstep
clinical_nodes.content: A forwarded email from a board-certified critical care pharmacist (via ACCP CC PRN) argues that because ACE2 is the SARS-CoV-2 receptor, ACE inhibitors and ARBs could be a therapeutic solution. These medications are later proven not to be effective.
clinical_nodes.preview: A forwarded email from a board-certified critical care pharmacist (via ACCP CC PRN) argues that because ACE2 is the SARS-CoV-2 receptor, ACE inhibitors and ARBs could be a therapeutic solution. These medications are later proven not to be effective.

sections.heading: Wide-Ranging Theories Among Highly Qualified Experts
sections.text: This is worth highlighting not because the reasoning was foolish — it wasn't. ACE2 as the viral entry receptor was established biology, and working forward from that to a potential therapeutic angle was the kind of mechanistic thinking that experts are supposed to do. But it illustrates how wide-ranging the theorizing was, even among deeply credentialed people. A board-certified critical care pharmacist, drawing on real science, arrived at a hypothesis that didn't hold up. That was the environment: everyone reasoning from first principles, in every direction, simultaneously.

sections.heading: Some Proved Correct, Most Did Not
sections.text: ACEi and ARBs weren't the only theories circulating at this level. There were hypotheses about anticoagulation, about statins, about famotidine, about vitamin D — some with mechanistic rationale, some with early observational signals, most ultimately going nowhere. A handful proved correct or at least useful. The lesson isn't that experts were wrong to theorize. It's that in a vacuum of evidence, even rigorous thinking produces a lot of noise alongside the signal. Sorting one from the other took time and trials that most institutions weren't running.

clinical_nodes.label: CAP Antibiotics Added to Covid-19 Order Set
clinical_nodes.content: Community-acquired pneumonia antibiotics added to the Covid-19 order set. Doxycycline prioritized as azithromycin supply dwindles.
clinical_nodes.preview: Community-acquired pneumonia antibiotics added to the Covid-19 order set. Doxycycline prioritized as azithromycin supply dwindles.

sections.heading: The Discomfort Was Real
sections.text: Many of us — myself included — felt uncomfortable with this. Empiric antibiotics for a viral pneumonia cut against everything antimicrobial stewardship stands for. The data for bacterial co-infection in Covid-19 was thin, and we knew it. But raising that concern in the middle of a surge, when people were dying in large numbers, felt impossible. Arguing for an abstract concept like antimicrobial resistance while clinicians were watching patients deteriorate in front of them was, in the moment, out of touch. So the antibiotics went in.

sections.heading: An Underappreciated Consequence
sections.text: The discomfort turned out to be warranted. Antibiotic overuse for Covid-19 became a massive problem in 2020, likely contributing to significant increases in antibiotic-resistant infections — a downstream harm that received far less attention than the virus itself. The argument that felt out of touch in March was the right argument. It just didn't have a place at the table when the immediate crisis was all anyone could focus on.

clinical_nodes.label: Testing Delays at Peak PUI Volume
clinical_nodes.content: Covid-19 test turnaround time is 3–5 days. This is the peak of persons under investigation — patients held in limbo, consuming isolation resources, while the system waits for results.
clinical_nodes.preview: Covid-19 test turnaround time is 3–5 days. This is the peak of persons under investigation — patients held in limbo, consuming isolation resources, while the system waits for results.

sections.heading: Blind in Uncharted Waters
sections.text: On top of everything else — the evolving treatment landscape, the conflicting guidance, the supply shortages — we were also operating largely blind to who was actually infected. A 3–5 day turnaround meant patients sat in limbo for days, consuming isolation resources and clinical bandwidth, before anyone knew for certain what they were dealing with. In the early stages of the pandemic, testing limitations were just another layer of uncertainty piled onto an already completely uncharted situation. You couldn't see the scope of what you were managing. You could only react to what was directly in front of you.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Duration Curtailed
clinical_nodes.content: Greater than 5-day HCQ duration discouraged due to limited evidence and supply constraints.

clinical_nodes.label: Azithromycin Recommendation Challenged
clinical_nodes.content: My ID PharmD colleague and I draft an SBAR to pull azithromycin from the protocol citing: no prior evidence, non-equivalent control group, no clinical benefit demonstrated, and potential QTc toxicity.
clinical_nodes.preview: My ID PharmD colleague and I draft an SBAR to pull azithromycin from the protocol citing: no prior evidence, non-equivalent control group, no clinical benefit demonstrated, and potential QTc toxicity.

sections.heading: A Shifting Tide
sections.text: This SBAR wasn't drafted in isolation. By late March, concern about azithromycin's inclusion in HCQ protocols was building across the country — pharmacists and physicians raising the same questions independently: where was the evidence for the combination? What did the French study actually show? Was the QTc risk being taken seriously enough? We were participating in a shifting tide, not starting one. The critiques we put into the SBAR reflected a growing consensus among people who had looked carefully at the same paper and arrived at the same uncomfortable conclusions.

sections.heading: Saying It Out Loud
sections.text: What the SBAR did was formalize the argument in our own institution. Citing the methodological weaknesses — no equivalent control group, no demonstrated clinical benefit, a supply shortage already straining the system — and putting it in writing was a way of forcing the conversation. It was uncomfortable to push back on something that had already been adopted and that physicians were actively using. But the concerns were real, they were shared widely, and they deserved to be on the record.

embed.label: Read the Analysis on Contagion Live

clinical_nodes.label: Remdesivir Compassionate Use
clinical_nodes.content: Gilead approves compassionate use remdesivir for a pregnant patient in the ICU. An emergency C-section is performed in the ICU before the drug arrives.

embed.label: Read the Story in the Washington Post

clinical_nodes.label: "There Are No Rules Anymore"
clinical_nodes.preview: A day of unproven therapies: ribavirin, zinc, colchicine, cyclosporine. I put it simply: "There are no rules anymore."
clinical_nodes.content: A wide-ranging day of unproven therapies. Ribavirin, zinc, colchicine, cyclosporine — none proven, all in play.

sections.heading: Everything Was in Play
sections.text: I add ribavirin to the reported inventory based on a contact of an ID physician in Egypt. Zinc sulfate shortages require active management — not recommended, not proven, but used widely. A conversation with my ID pharmacy colleague covers zinc + HCQ, colchicine, and cyclosporine: the first time I have heard of colchicine for Covid-19, the first time he has heard of cyclosporine.

sections.heading: No Rules
sections.text: That's how I put it: 'There are no rules anymore.' Every day brought new therapies to evaluate — most with little or no evidence, all with desperate clinical rationale. The normal framework for how drugs make it into practice had collapsed. We weren't recommending these therapies. We were just trying to keep up with them.

clinical_nodes.label: Tocilizumab Outcomes Concerning
clinical_nodes.content: Suspicion that tocilizumab is being used too late — very few positive outcomes observed.

clinical_nodes.label: Hub-and-Spoke ICU Strategy
clinical_nodes.content: A hub-and-spoke strategy is established for ICU services in response to a massive surge in critically ill patients. ICU, Anesthesia, Pediatrics, and Burn services all take over adult Covid-19 ICUs.

clinical_nodes.label: Universal Masking Begins
clinical_nodes.content: Universal masking goes into effect at CBMC.
clinical_nodes.preview: Universal masking goes into effect at CBMC.

sections.heading: Where I Stood on Masking
sections.text: There's a lot to be said about the masking debate. I generally followed CDC guidance throughout — I didn't have the bandwidth to dig deeply into the primary literature on my own. On the whole, I think the guidance was accurate and correct. With one significant exception.

sections.heading: The Early Messaging Problem
sections.text: Early in the pandemic, in an effort to prevent panic buying and preserve supply for frontline clinicians, there was a suggestion — implicit or explicit depending on who was communicating it — that masks didn't work for the general public. The intent was understandable. The result was damaging. When the guidance shifted and masks became universally recommended, it looked like the CDC had changed its position. To many people, it felt like they had been misled. That perception of a shifting message created a deficit of trust that public health officials spent years trying to recover from.

sections.heading: How Difficult It Is to Message in a Crisis
sections.text: This is one of the clearest examples from the pandemic of how hard public health communication actually is. The decision to downplay masks early may have been made with good intentions — protect the supply chain, prevent runs on PPE. But it produced a long-term cost that likely exceeded whatever short-term benefit it achieved. When credibility is your most important asset, a message that appears to shift puts you in a very difficult place. It's a lesson that deserves more reflection than it has gotten.

clinical_nodes.label: Guidelines Updated Again
clinical_nodes.content: Updated guidelines: HCQ duration extended to 5-7 days, tocilizumab criteria broadened, anticoagulation and steroids formally addressed.

clinical_nodes.label: First Baricitinib Question
clinical_nodes.content: A pharmacist at NBI asks about baricitinib — my first encounter with it as a potential immunomodulator for Covid-19. Speculation about its efficacy had appeared in a Lancet study. It is proven effective months later.

embed.label: Read the Study in The Lancet

clinical_nodes.label: IRB Chair: Trial Enrollment vs. Compassionate Use
clinical_nodes.content: The IRB Chair will only recruit to the sarilumab study if tocilizumab is unavailable — because his study has a placebo arm. A stance that cuts to a core ethical tension of the pandemic.
clinical_nodes.preview: The IRB Chair will only recruit to the sarilumab study if tocilizumab is unavailable — because his study has a placebo arm. A stance that cuts to a core ethical tension of the pandemic.

sections.heading: Option A vs. Option B
sections.text: Option A: enroll a patient in a randomized clinical trial, where they may be assigned to drug or placebo. Option B: decline trial enrollment and simply give them the unproven drug. Option A moves us closer to an answer. Option B does not. No patient deserves judgment for decisions made under the stress of severe illness. But if COVID-19 persists, we will only know which treatments work because of patients who chose Option A — and chose to drive medicine forward for the greater good.

sections.heading: Did the IRB Chair Get It Wrong?
sections.text: I can't help but think the answer is yes — at least in principle. If we didn't actually know whether tocilizumab worked, then a patient's first option should have been trial enrollment, not compassionate use. Withholding trial participation until the drug was unavailable inverts that priority. The whole point of the trial was to find out. That said, tocilizumab did prove to have benefit in specific scenarios — so there's a reasonable argument that the IRB Chair's instinct to offer a known option first wasn't entirely wrong. It's an honest tension, and I'm not sure it has a clean resolution.

personal_nodes.label: A Family Member's 50th Birthday Skipped
personal_nodes.content: After another family member decided not to attend first, my wife and I follow their lead and skip a 50th birthday gathering. The family member's parents later end up in the ICU; her father passes away.
personal_nodes.preview: After another family member decided not to attend first, my wife and I follow their lead and skip a 50th birthday gathering. The family member's parents later end up in the ICU; her father passes away.

sections.heading: Following Someone Else's Lead
sections.text: I want to be honest about how this decision happened. It wasn't that I independently assessed the risk and made a call. Another family member said no first. I followed that lead. The right thing happened, but it happened because someone else acted first. That distinction matters to me.

sections.heading: A Question I Can't Answer
sections.text: Her parents ended up in the ICU. Her father didn't survive. I don't know if the party is what led to their infection. I may never know. But I can't help wondering. It's the kind of question that doesn't have a clean resolution — just a possibility that sits there, unverifiable, alongside everything else from that period.

personal_nodes.label: Ibuprofen: Impossible to Disprove
personal_nodes.content: A single premature warning — echoed around the world in hours. The corrections and retractions never travel with the same intensity.
personal_nodes.preview: A single premature warning — echoed around the world in hours. The corrections and retractions never travel with the same intensity.

sections.heading: The Misinformation Cycle
sections.text: This blew up on social media over the next few weeks, especially after the WHO picked up on it. It became a frequent question — at work, and outside of work with friends and family. Level-headed questions like 'what's the source for this claim?' got lost in the noise. Proving the absence of evidence is impossible, while the provocative rumor spreads faster than a virus. And the corrections and retractions never travel with the intensity of the original report. When this cycle repeats over and over, the result is a flood of misinformation.

sections.heading: You Can't Broadcast Speculation
sections.text: This episode drove home something I've thought about a lot since: the responsibility that comes with a platform, however large or small. When you are in a leadership position — as a public health official, a clinician, a communicator — people extend you a degree of trust that amplifies whatever you say. Broadcasting speculation as if it were guidance doesn't just spread one wrong idea. It erodes the credibility of the entire information environment. People start to wonder what else they've been told that wasn't actually known. The standard has to be: if you don't know, say you don't know. Speculation belongs in a hypothesis, not a headline.

personal_nodes.label: My Wife Works From Home
personal_nodes.content: My wife begins working from home. Initially the expectation is that it will be a few weeks. Five years later, she's never gone back.

personal_nodes.label: Reading Chinese Papers
personal_nodes.content: 'Trying to read all of these Chinese papers' — I use Google Translate on foreign-language studies to keep up with global evidence.
personal_nodes.preview: 'Trying to read all of these Chinese papers' — I use Google Translate on foreign-language studies to keep up with global evidence.

sections.heading: The Most Extreme Example
sections.text: Running Chinese-language studies through Google Translate to find clinical signals isn't a workflow anyone would design on purpose. But that's where the early data was — case series and observational reports out of Wuhan, Hubei, and Beijing, published in Chinese journals before the global medical community had caught up. I mention it here not because it was the norm, but because it's the clearest example of the lengths I was willing to go to. If there was a paper that might tell us something useful, I was going to find a way to read it.

sections.heading: What That Period Actually Looked Like
sections.text: Spending evenings and weekends combing PubMed, medRxiv, bioRxiv, and whatever emerged from overseas health ministries. Reading everything I could get my hands on — preprints, case reports, letters to the editor, hospital protocols shared informally through the ID Pharmacy Collaborative. The translated papers were just the outer edge of that effort. The goal was simple: know more than I knew yesterday, and use it to keep our guidelines as current as possible.

personal_nodes.label: NJ Closes Nonessential Businesses
personal_nodes.content: New Jersey closes nonessential businesses.

personal_nodes.label: Emails From Strangers
personal_nodes.content: I receive an email from a new attending physician about the guidelines. Over the following weeks, I receive emails from physicians in NYC and India — both unaffiliated with our health system — because the guidelines included my contact info.

personal_nodes.label: Celebrating a Preprint With Amy
personal_nodes.content: A preprint RCT from China suggests benefit with hydroxychloroquine. My first real encounter with preprints — a format I'd become very familiar with.
personal_nodes.preview: A preprint RCT from China suggests benefit with hydroxychloroquine. My first real encounter with preprints — a format I'd become very familiar with.

sections.heading: The Price of Speed
sections.text: Preprints come from good intentions: rapid sharing of study results. But there is a price to skipping the steps that ensure the slow but forward march of scientific progress. That price is confusion, when the important studies get mixed with those that are far more suspect — and that confusion is magnified when preprints of questionable merit are shared widely by media outlets. I never confirmed whether this paper was ever published in a peer-reviewed journal.

personal_nodes.label: The Offensive Coordinator
personal_nodes.content: My role doesn't have me at the bedside. I work more like a football team's offensive coordinator — except less controlling and more advisory.
personal_nodes.preview: My role doesn't have me at the bedside. I work more like a football team's offensive coordinator — except less controlling and more advisory.

sections.heading: Two Opposing Pressures
sections.text: On one side: the importance of evidence-based medicine, where no practice should be recommended without adequate evidence. On the other: a surge of patients that won't wait for high-quality trials. By any nonpandemic standard, there is not adequate evidence to recommend for or against many COVID-19 therapies. But this nonanswer provides no help to the clinicians on the front lines, swamped with patients, looking for some kind of direction.

personal_nodes.label: Video Call With My Wife
personal_nodes.content: My wife and I sit on our balcony and talk for 30 minutes. We know it's worth documenting, so we record it.
personal_nodes.preview: My wife and I sit on our balcony and talk for 30 minutes. We know it's worth documenting, so we record it.

sections.heading: A Snapshot of April 2020
sections.text: My wife is grocery shopping online. The niece and nephew are 'living their best life making mud soup.' My wife's knuckles are cracked from hand sanitizing. I'm not yet wearing a mask at work — the CDC hadn't recommended it for healthcare workers yet. 'We're just a covid hospital, every unit is an ICU, no one knows how to treat it, people want to try everything.' 'My music selection is a good indicator of my mental state — I've been listening to emo like it's 2005.'

chapters.title: The Dark Ages
chapters.subtitle: The Surge, the Silence, and the Trailers
chapters.mood_description: The hospital empties of non-essential staff but fills with the dying. Empty hallways. Refrigerated trailers in the parking lot. Clap-outs. 'Here Comes the Sun.' Tooth-grinding stress. An overwhelming sense of hopelessness on the morning commute.
chapters.introduction: The hospital filled with the dying and emptied of almost everyone else. Hallways went quiet. Refrigerated trailers appeared in the parking lot. I moved into a hotel to protect my family and started grinding my teeth without realizing it. This was the stretch where hopelessness stopped being an abstract concept and became something I felt on my morning commute.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Skepticism Grows
clinical_nodes.content: A NEJM quasi-experimental HCQ study circulates. The first serious discussion in the ID pharmacy group of pulling the HCQ recommendation entirely. The group notes that stopping azithromycin 'wasn't as bad as expected.'
clinical_nodes.preview: A NEJM quasi-experimental HCQ study circulates. The first serious discussion in the ID pharmacy group of pulling the HCQ recommendation entirely. The group notes that stopping azithromycin 'wasn't as bad as expected.'

sections.heading: The Weight of Changing Direction
sections.text: We felt an incredible sense of responsibility. Recommending a drug and then pulling it isn't a neutral act — it has real implications for patients who were treated, for clinicians who prescribed it based on our guidance, and for the trust that makes guidelines worth following in the first place. Shifting direction felt like a huge deal every single time. Whether clinicians recognized the complexity of operating in that environment, or simply didn't have the bandwidth to push back, I honestly don't know. But the pushback was minimal.

sections.heading: We Weren't an Outlier
sections.text: It's also worth saying that our guidance didn't differ substantially from national trends. We followed the evidence as it developed, moved when the major guidelines moved, and didn't stray far from what NIH, IDSA, and other major bodies were saying. The HCQ arc — early adoption, growing skepticism, eventual removal — played out the same way across institutions throughout the country. We weren't ahead of it, but we weren't behind it either.

clinical_nodes.label: Ivermectin: First and Last (for 8 months)
clinical_nodes.content: Two ID physicians separately ask about ivermectin. I explain that concentrations required for efficacy are orders of magnitude above what can be safely achieved. No more ivermectin questions for eight months.
clinical_nodes.preview: Two ID physicians separately ask about ivermectin. I explain that concentrations required for efficacy are orders of magnitude above what can be safely achieved. No more ivermectin questions for eight months.

sections.heading: Standing on Others' Work
sections.text: To be clear, the pharmacokinetic argument wasn't my own original analysis — I was sharing work that others in the field had already done. Researchers had modeled the concentrations required to achieve antiviral efficacy based on in vitro data and found them to be orders of magnitude above what could safely be achieved in a human being. I read that analysis, understood it, and used it to answer the question. That's how it should work — you don't have to derive everything from scratch, you have to know where to look and be able to evaluate what you find.

clinical_nodes.label: Tocilizumab: Earlier May Be Better
clinical_nodes.content: Preliminary tocilizumab results suggest earlier administration may be more effective — consistent with our clinical suspicion.
clinical_nodes.preview: Preliminary tocilizumab results suggest earlier administration may be more effective — consistent with our clinical suspicion.

sections.heading: A Major Focus
sections.text: Tocilizumab was one of the things I was most absorbed by during this period. The drug was being used, but we genuinely didn't know how to use it — who benefited, when they should get it, what the right window was. Frontline clinicians were asking for guidance, and there wasn't much to give them. The published data was thin and the clinical picture was murky. So we did what we could: we started analyzing what we had.

sections.heading: Days, Nights, and Weekends
sections.text: I worked on this constantly — evenings, weekends, whatever time I could find outside of the regular day. I want to be clear that it was nothing compared to what the clinicians were doing. They were doing the same kind of sustained, exhausting work, except theirs was directly at the bedside with critically ill patients. But this was the most meaningful way I could contribute. If I could get the data together fast enough to actually be useful, it was worth the hours.

sections.heading: Share It As Soon As You Have Something
sections.text: As soon as we had preliminary results, we shared them. The finding — that earlier treatment appeared to be more effective — aligned with what some clinicians had already been suspecting based on what they were seeing at the bedside. That clinical intuition was right, and having data to support it mattered. Not because it settled the question definitively, but because it gave people something to work with while the field was still sorting itself out.

clinical_nodes.label: NIH Guidelines Adopted
clinical_nodes.content: Our AMS decides guidelines will mirror NIH guidelines going forward — requiring a much higher evidence threshold before making recommendations. No recommended therapies at this point.
clinical_nodes.preview: Our AMS decides guidelines will mirror NIH guidelines going forward — requiring a much higher evidence threshold before making recommendations. No recommended therapies at this point.

sections.heading: A Response to the Missteps
sections.text: This decision didn't happen in a vacuum. It was shaped, at least in part, by what had happened with HCQ — a case study in what it looks like when guidance gets built on very little evidence and turns out to be wrong. We had recommended a drug, watched the evidence deteriorate, and walked it back. That experience had a cost — to credibility, to trust, to the clinicians who had followed our recommendations in good faith. Adopting NIH guidelines as our standard was, in some ways, a direct response to that.

sections.heading: Humility Over Speed
sections.text: The new standard required a much higher evidence threshold before we'd make a recommendation. The practical effect was stark: at the point of adoption, there were no recommended therapies. That was the honest answer, and it was a hard one to give. But it was the right call. Moving fast on weak evidence had produced guidance that ultimately did harm. Being more cautious, more humble about what we actually knew — even when clinicians wanted answers we couldn't give — was the more defensible position. And the more responsible one.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Pulled From Guidelines
clinical_nodes.content: The HCQ recommendation is officially removed from our guidelines based on NIH and IDSA guidance.

clinical_nodes.label: Infection Paradox: CDI Down, Fungal Infections Up
clinical_nodes.content: Hospital CDI rates plummet from improved hand hygiene — while fungal infections, MRSA, and hospital-acquired infections rise in the overwhelmed ICU. The same pandemic that suppresses some diseases worsens others.
clinical_nodes.preview: Hospital CDI rates plummet from improved hand hygiene — while fungal infections, MRSA, and hospital-acquired infections rise in the overwhelmed ICU. The same pandemic that suppresses some diseases worsens others.

sections.heading: Suppression and Emergence
sections.text: The same pattern held for respiratory illnesses — influenza, RSV, and rhinovirus transmission collapsed as masking and social distancing spread. Other infectious diseases would remain suppressed throughout the pandemic. Meanwhile, the surge of critically ill patients with prolonged stays drove dramatic increases in fungal and drug-resistant bacterial infections.

clinical_nodes.label: All Hands on Deck
clinical_nodes.content: As the surge overwhelms the hospital, everyone steps outside their training. The pediatric intensivist manages adult ICU patients. The investigational drug pharmacist becomes a critical care pharmacist.
clinical_nodes.preview: As the surge overwhelms the hospital, everyone steps outside their training. The pediatric intensivist manages adult ICU patients. The investigational drug pharmacist becomes a critical care pharmacist.

sections.heading: Not Because They Want To
sections.text: Medical students are called into action in areas of care they were never prepared for. More and more is asked of healthcare workers — and each time, they step up to the task. Not because they want to, but because they have to, for their patients.

clinical_nodes.label: Remdesivir: Contradictory Data
clinical_nodes.content: A Chinese remdesivir RCT in Lancet shows no benefit. The same day, NIAID shares preliminary RCT data indicating benefit. Considerable confusion — but the Lancet study was underpowered.
clinical_nodes.preview: A Chinese remdesivir RCT in Lancet shows no benefit. The same day, NIAID shares preliminary RCT data indicating benefit. Considerable confusion — but the Lancet study was underpowered.

sections.heading: You Can't Call the Game Early
sections.text: The Lancet study fell short of its projected patient enrollment. Drawing definitive conclusions from an underpowered study is like calling a football game at the end of the third quarter. Congratulations to the 49ers, Super Bowl LIV champions — they were up 10, it was over, right? Wrong. There was still a quarter left to play.

sections.heading: Why Was There a Press Conference?
sections.text: In retrospect, the timing of the NIAID announcement feels intentional. The Lancet study showing no benefit published that morning. The same day, the White House held a press conference announcing the NIAID data showing benefit — before the full study was published or available for scrutiny. At the time, it was confusing. Later, reading CDC guidance on public health communication, one point stood out: the importance of being the first voice on an issue. The general consensus among people who analyzed this period was that the timing was not a coincidence. The press conference was designed to shape the narrative before the Lancet findings could dominate it.

clinical_nodes.label: Remdesivir EUA
clinical_nodes.content: FDA issues emergency use authorization for remdesivir.

clinical_nodes.label: "Do We Have Remdesivir? If Not, Why Not?"
clinical_nodes.preview: The US government distributes remdesivir — no RWJBH hospitals are selected. A reporter contacts CBMC on behalf of a patient: "Do we have remdesivir? If not, why not?"
clinical_nodes.content: The US government distributes remdesivir — no RWJBH hospitals are selected. A reporter contacts CBMC on behalf of a patient asking why not.

sections.heading: Passed Over
sections.text: The US government begins distributing remdesivir — and no RWJBH hospitals are selected for the initial shipment. We are told it was sent to the hardest-hit areas, but it is unclear how most of New Jersey was passed over. Two NJ sites received it; both returned their allocations to the state, which assembled a committee to determine redistribution.

sections.heading: A Reporter Calls
sections.text: A reporter from ABC, working on behalf of a patient, reaches out to CBMC Public Relations. The inquiry is directed to Pharmacy and to me through the IRB Chair: 'Do we have remdesivir? If not, why not?'

clinical_nodes.label: Anakinra Early Data
clinical_nodes.content: First retrospective cohort data on anakinra published in The Lancet. I note it 'looks better than toci data.' Tocilizumab use is dropping significantly.

embed.label: Read the Study in The Lancet Rheumatology

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ NEJM Study: No Benefit
clinical_nodes.content: A large observational study published in NEJM finds no benefit from hydroxychloroquine.

clinical_nodes.label: What Is Our Threshold to Act Before National Guidelines?
clinical_nodes.preview: I ask the CPO and AMS physician lead: what is our threshold to act before national guidelines? The answer: we follow national guidelines. It was the right call — but colleagues wanted to be more nimble. The leaders chose to stand firm.
clinical_nodes.content:
I email the RWJBH CPO and AMS physician lead: "What is the threshold for modifying our recommendations before national guidelines?" The answer: we will follow national guidelines.

The question anticipates a recurring tension — new data that shifts the treatment landscape days or weeks before guidelines catch up. This will eventually come to a head most visibly with tocilizumab, leaving clinicians wanting to act on emerging evidence with no institutional guidance to support them.

sections.heading: The Right Call, With a Real Cost
sections.text: Following NIH guidelines was the correct institutional response — a higher evidence threshold, less exposure to the risk of recommending something that later proved wrong. I understood that, and I agreed with it. But in practice, it meant that when new data emerged that pointed clearly in a direction, our hands were tied until the national guidelines caught up. That gap — sometimes days, sometimes weeks — became a source of genuine friction. We knew enough to have an opinion. The answer was: wait.

sections.heading: Nimble vs. Accountable
sections.text: Colleagues who were deep in the literature, reading the same emerging evidence I was, wanted to act. The impulse was understandable: the data looked promising, patients needed treatment, why wait? The leaders — the CPO, the AMS physician lead — held firm. They chose institutional accountability over clinical nimbleness. They were right to. But for the clinicians managing patients day to day, wanting to offer something they believed in, it was a hard position to be in. The downside of standing firm is that you feel it most acutely at the bedside.

clinical_nodes.label: First Remdesivir Shipment
clinical_nodes.preview: 26 courses arrive at 6–7pm after a day-long delay. Allocation strategy debated, supply falls short of demand, and a Saturday morning CMO call is needed to approve orders.
clinical_nodes.content: 26 courses of remdesivir arrive after a day-long delay. Allocation, logistics, and implementation all come together in real time — nothing goes as planned.

sections.heading: First Come, First Served
sections.text: The CEO emails pharmacy leadership and me to sort out receiving logistics. We ask the CMO how to allocate a limited supply — 4–15 courses expected — against 25–30 anticipated eligible patients. The CMO responds: first come, first served. This becomes the system-wide strategy. I later hear from colleagues at other institutions that first come, first served is not considered equitable. We did not have the capacity to implement a more equitable allocation process.

sections.heading: Nothing Went as Planned
sections.text: Midday, the shipment is reported delayed. The next day, 26 courses arrive around 6–7pm. An ID physician pre-emptively enters roughly 10 orders that evening — after I have left for the day. We never established a clear implementation process. I end up calling the CMO on Saturday morning to review and approve the orders.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Gated to Clinical Trial
clinical_nodes.content: A P&T memo to Medical Staff states: HCQ for Covid-19 must go through a clinical trial. Non-trial use permitted only if a patient is ineligible. The ID physician responds calling it 'Tuskegee type illegal.'
clinical_nodes.preview: A P&T memo to Medical Staff states: HCQ for Covid-19 must go through a clinical trial. Non-trial use permitted only if a patient is ineligible. The ID physician responds calling it 'Tuskegee type illegal.'

sections.heading: A Policy With a Built-In Loophole
sections.text: The intent of the memo was clear: if HCQ was going to be used, it needed to generate evidence. Route it through a trial, or justify why the patient couldn't be enrolled. In theory, this was the right framework. In practice, it had no teeth. The trial never successfully enrolled patients — and once it became clear that enrollment wasn't happening, the exception clause became the rule. If there's no functioning trial to enroll into, every patient is effectively ineligible. The loophole was structural.

sections.heading: No Impact on the Contrarian
sections.text: The physician who had been the most vocal and persistent advocate for HCQ continued prescribing it. The policy that was meant to constrain non-evidence-based use had inadvertently given him a permanent workaround. We couldn't coordinate around a trial. We couldn't enforce enrollment. So the memo — however well-intentioned — ended up changing very little about what was actually happening at the bedside.

personal_nodes.label: Life During the Surge
personal_nodes.preview: Empty hallways, refrigerated trailers, clap-outs, tooth-grinding, and a hotel stay to protect my family. The human texture of the worst weeks.

sections.title: The Hospital Empties
sections.text: Non-essential personnel work from home, leaving the hospital eerily quiet — empty hallways, a silent cafeteria.

sections.title: Community Donations
sections.text: All kinds of donations arrived. Hand sanitizer from local distilleries that had converted their production lines. Food, drinks, snacks. Mask comfort adapters. Handwritten letters from entire classrooms of kids who had never met any of us. There was a period where you could feel the appreciation from the community — everyone wanted to do something, anything, to help. It meant something.

sections.title: The City Goes Quiet
sections.text: Outside the hospital, the world shut down. Parks, playgrounds, schools — all closed.

sections.title: Trailers in the Parking Lot
sections.text: The hospital morgue fills. Two refrigerated trailers are placed in the physician parking lot for overflow bodies. They remained for months. Every employee passed them going in and out each day.

sections.title: 'Here Comes the Sun'
sections.text: The Beatles' 'Here Comes the Sun' plays over the hospital loudspeaker each time a patient is extubated. The sound quality is described as horrendous.

sections.title: Clap-Outs
sections.text: Staff line the halls and applaud patients discharged after weeks- or months-long admissions.

sections.title: Hotel Stay
sections.text: I move into a hotel from April 7 to April 18 to protect my family from exposure.

sections.title: Tooth-Grinding
sections.text: I notice mild, persistent tooth pain — eventually realizing it is from clenching my jaw. A subconscious physical response to facing unanswerable questions with lives on the line.

sections.title: Morning Commute Grief
sections.text: 'I remember getting emotional a few times. It was always on my morning commute... I remember feeling an overwhelming hopelessness. Not just for myself and all the patients and their families. But for everyone I care about and how isolated and scared they must be.'

sections.title: My Wife's Knit Mask
sections.text: My wife handknits a mask for me to use outside the hospital, since there weren't enough for non-healthcare workers. 'It probably didn't work that well but it meant a lot to me that she worked on it for me.'

sections.title: My Wife Cuts My Hair
sections.text: My wife cuts my hair — three attempts, two hours — and it turns out well. I have jealous coworkers.

personal_nodes.label: Healthcare Heroes Salute
personal_nodes.content: A procession of thanks outside St. Barnabas — police, fire, and community honoring hospital staff at the height of the surge. A genuine high point of public appreciation.
personal_nodes.preview: A procession of thanks outside St. Barnabas — police, fire, and community honoring hospital staff at the height of the surge. A genuine high point of public appreciation.

sections.heading: A High Point of Appreciation
sections.text: This was genuinely moving. Police, fire, community members showing up outside the hospital at the height of the surge to say thank you — it meant something real to the people inside. The country was paying attention, and for a moment, the recognition felt proportionate to what was being asked of healthcare workers. This was the peak of that goodwill.

personal_nodes.label: Preparing for the Next Wave
personal_nodes.content: CBMC pharmacy featured in a Wall Street Journal article on hospitals stocking up on Covid-19 drugs ahead of a feared second wave in the fall.
