chapters.title: The MAB Graveyard
chapters.subtitle: The Monoclonal Antibody Arms Race
chapters.mood_description: A two-year arc of approved monoclonal antibodies — each arriving with promise, each eventually rendered obsolete by viral mutation. A clinical arms race the virus ultimately wins.
chapters.introduction: Over two years, we operationalized a succession of monoclonal antibodies — each one arriving with promise, each one eventually rendered obsolete by a virus that kept evolving. I was wrong about that. Early in the pandemic, I had dismissed concerns about meaningful viral mutation. The virus proved otherwise, and this chapter is in many ways a record of that reckoning.

clinical_nodes.label: Sarilumab Fails Primary Endpoint
clinical_nodes.content: Regeneron reports that sarilumab fails its primary endpoint. The study criteria were too broad — later analyses would prove the drug effective in more targeted scenarios.
clinical_nodes.preview: Regeneron reports that sarilumab fails its primary endpoint. The study criteria were too broad — later analyses would prove the drug effective in more targeted scenarios.

sections.heading: What Happened
sections.text: The sarilumab trial enrolled too wide a range of patients, including those in circumstances where the drug was unlikely to help. When the primary endpoint failed, the headline was 'sarilumab doesn't work' — even though the drug itself wasn't the problem. Regeneron simultaneously pivoted to a monoclonal antibody targeting the spike protein, which would eventually become casirivimab/imdevimab.

sections.heading: Asking the Right Question
sections.text: This is one of the clearest examples of how difficult it is to find effective treatments in the midst of a pandemic. It is not enough to have the right therapy — you also have to ask the right question. That means testing it in the correct patient population, at the right point in the disease course, with the right outcome measure. Get any one of those wrong, and a drug that works can look like one that doesn't.

sections.heading: The Cost of Conflicting Messages
sections.text: For those of us trying to translate the evidence in real time, this was deeply frustrating. When the study failed, we stopped recommending sarilumab. When later data showed benefit in a more targeted population, we had to reverse course. This pattern — told a therapy doesn't work, then told it does — added to the exhaustion of navigating the pandemic. Each revision eroded a little more trust, both in the science and in those communicating it.

embed.label: Read the Coverage on Clinical Trials Arena

clinical_nodes.label: First Compassionate Use: Casirivimab/Imdevimab
clinical_nodes.content: I work with the Pediatric ID physician on the first (of at least two) compassionate use requests for Regeneron's casirivimab/imdevimab in a pediatric patient.

clinical_nodes.label: The President Receives Casirivimab/Imdevimab
clinical_nodes.content: The President publicly discloses he has Covid-19 and receives casirivimab/imdevimab via compassionate use. This brings considerable media attention to the medication.
clinical_nodes.preview: The President publicly discloses he has Covid-19 and receives casirivimab/imdevimab via compassionate use. This brings considerable media attention to the medication.

sections.heading: A Question of Access
sections.text: The treatment the President received was real — casirivimab/imdevimab was available at that time, but only through compassionate use. That pathway exists for individual patients with serious or life-threatening conditions when no comparable alternatives are available. It is not a broad access program. The public saw a high-profile patient receive a cutting-edge therapy and reasonably asked: why can't we get that?

sections.heading: What I Knew
sections.text: From where I sat, the answer was straightforward: compassionate use is a narrow channel. We had already navigated it ourselves — the first request I worked on for this same drug came just days earlier. It required significant coordination: a physician request, IRB notification, and direct negotiation with the manufacturer. The drug wasn't being withheld. It simply wasn't available at scale yet, and access required going through a process most patients and providers didn't know existed.

sections.heading: The Perception Problem
sections.text: But that explanation doesn't land easily when the most powerful person in the country appears to receive preferential treatment during a crisis. The optics amplified existing frustrations about equity and access. For those of us in the field, it was a reminder that scientific accuracy and public trust are not the same thing — and that a single high-profile case can shape perception in ways that months of careful communication cannot undo.

embed.label: Read the Story on STAT News

clinical_nodes.label: Bamlanivimab EUA Granted
clinical_nodes.content: FDA grants EUA for bamlanivimab. Immediate discussions about where to administer it — a pharmacy colleague and I suggest the ED. The system decides on EDs by November 16.
clinical_nodes.preview: FDA grants EUA for bamlanivimab. Immediate discussions about where to administer it — a pharmacy colleague and I suggest the ED. The system decides on EDs by November 16.

sections.heading: When Pharmacy Became Late Night
sections.text: Getting a new medication to the bedside involves a lot of unglamorous work — formulary review, protocol development, ordering infrastructure, staff education, storage logistics. I had been doing this for nearly ten years. It was important, meticulous work that almost no one outside the hospital ever thought about. Then bamlanivimab got its EUA, and it became a late-night talk show bit. Watching a medication I was operationalizing in real time get discussed on television was one of the stranger experiences of the pandemic. A useful reminder of just how far outside the ordinary we had traveled.

sections.heading: ED vs. Clinic: A Structural Choice
sections.text: Many health systems stood up dedicated infusion clinics to administer monoclonal antibodies — a cleaner setup on paper, purpose-built for this kind of outpatient therapy. We didn't have that infrastructure readily available. So we went to the ED. That decision made our emergency departments busier, and it wasn't without friction. But it also meant we weren't dependent on a fixed clinic model when the landscape kept shifting. Over the next two years, as different monoclonals came and went — new variants, changing efficacy, supply disruptions — that nimbleness turned out to matter. We could pivot quickly because we weren't locked into a structure built around a single product.

embed.label: Watch the Late Night Segment on YouTube

clinical_nodes.label: Bamlanivimab Dispensing Begins
clinical_nodes.content: Our hospital begins dispensing bamlanivimab.

clinical_nodes.label: Casirivimab/Imdevimab EUA
clinical_nodes.content: FDA grants EUA for casirivimab/imdevimab (Regeneron). Both 'bam' and 'Regeneron' are used simultaneously based on inconsistent supply.

clinical_nodes.label: Bamlanivimab/Etesevimab EUA
clinical_nodes.content: FDA grants EUA for the combination. Rationale: resistance to bamlanivimab suspected in circulating variants; etesevimab intended to restore activity. Variant monitoring becomes essential to treatment decisions.
clinical_nodes.preview: FDA grants EUA for the combination. Rationale: resistance to bamlanivimab suspected in circulating variants; etesevimab intended to restore activity. Variant monitoring becomes essential to treatment decisions.

sections.heading: A Weekly Ritual
sections.text: From this point forward, the CDC nowcast — weekly estimates of variant proportions circulating nationally and regionally — became a standing appointment. Every week, I was checking which variants were dominant, what their resistance profiles looked like against available monoclonals, and what that meant for what we could actually offer patients. It was a level of real-time pharmacoepidemiology that simply hadn't existed before in clinical practice.

sections.heading: Remarkable, With a Catch
sections.text: Stepping back, it was genuinely extraordinary that we had weekly surveillance data at all — that a national infrastructure existed to estimate variant proportions and publish them publicly, in near real time. No prior pandemic had anything like it. But we also knew its limits: the nowcast lagged. It reflected what had been circulating in the weeks prior, not necessarily what was in your hospital, in your county, on the day you were making the decision. You were always navigating with a map drawn slightly in the past.

clinical_nodes.label: Mutation Reckoning
clinical_nodes.content: I reflect on my prior dismissal of mutation concerns: 'This was very wrong.' My first concern was the Alpha variant (UK), more transmissible but not affecting MAB activity. Then variants emerged worldwide.
clinical_nodes.preview: I reflect on my prior dismissal of mutation concerns: 'This was very wrong.' My first concern was the Alpha variant (UK), more transmissible but not affecting MAB activity. Then variants emerged worldwide.

sections.heading: What I Had Read
sections.text: Early in the pandemic, I did a significant amount of reading on coronavirus biology — trying to get my bearings like everyone else. One of the prevailing opinions at the time was that SARS-related coronaviruses were relatively stable, that their mutation rate was lower than influenza, and that dramatic antigenic drift was unlikely. That framing was not unreasonable given what was known about the original SARS-CoV-1. I absorbed it, and it shaped my thinking.

sections.heading: Dismissing the Concerns
sections.text: Friends and colleagues raised questions early on about variants and whether the virus would evolve around our countermeasures. I pushed back. I had read the literature; I thought I understood the biology. The scientific consensus I had encountered gave me a confidence that, in retrospect, I held too tightly. SARS-CoV-2 turned out to be far more capable of meaningful evolution than that early framing suggested — not in the same way as influenza, but in ways that mattered enormously for treatment.

sections.heading: Being Wrong
sections.text: Acknowledging this isn't just an intellectual exercise. My dismissal of mutation concerns had practical consequences — it shaped how I thought about the durability of our monoclonal antibody strategies in the early months. The virus eventually rendered nearly every monoclonal antibody we had clinically obsolete. That is not a failure of science; science updated as evidence emerged. But it is a reminder that reading widely is not the same as reading correctly, and that confident early opinions in a fast-moving situation deserve more humility than I gave them.

clinical_nodes.label: Bamlanivimab Alone EUA Revoked
clinical_nodes.content: EUA for single-agent bamlanivimab revoked due to variant resistance.
clinical_nodes.preview: EUA for single-agent bamlanivimab revoked due to variant resistance.

sections.heading: Supply vs. Activity: An Ethical Tightrope
sections.text: As the variant landscape shifted, so did the choices in front of us — and they weren't always clean ones. At certain points we had one monoclonal with greater supply but a higher likelihood of resistance, and another with stronger activity against circulating variants but limited availability. Was it ethical to give the more accessible drug when it was less likely to work? Was it ethical to reserve the better drug for patients who could wait, when another patient was in front of you now? These weren't hypotheticals. They were real decisions, made in real time, without good precedent.

sections.heading: When There's Only One Option Left
sections.text: There were periods when we were down to a single available monoclonal, and resistance to it was likely given the variants circulating. And yet patients and providers still wanted to do something. The desire to act is deeply human — and deeply understandable. But offering a therapy with probable resistance meant weighing a marginal chance of benefit against the signal we were sending: that we had tools when we may not have. These were some of the harder conversations of the entire monoclonal era.

clinical_nodes.label: Sotrovimab EUA
clinical_nodes.content: FDA grants EUA for sotrovimab.

clinical_nodes.label: Casirivimab/Imdevimab and Bamlanivimab/Etesevimab EUAs Revoked
clinical_nodes.content: Both EUAs revoked due to variant resistance.

clinical_nodes.label: Bebtelovimab EUA
clinical_nodes.content: FDA grants EUA for bebtelovimab despite 'basically no clinical data.'

clinical_nodes.label: Sotrovimab EUA Restricted
clinical_nodes.content: Sotrovimab EUA revised to exclude areas with high BA.2 prevalence, including New Jersey. Later fully revoked.

clinical_nodes.label: Bebtelovimab EUA Revoked — The Virus Wins
clinical_nodes.content: Bebtelovimab, the last available monoclonal antibody, has its EUA revoked. 'The virus has won the arms race.'
clinical_nodes.preview: Bebtelovimab, the last available monoclonal antibody, has its EUA revoked. 'The virus has won the arms race.'

sections.heading: What We Had Done
sections.text: The virus winning the arms race was real — but it was only part of the story. In the span of two years, our system had operationalized a succession of monoclonal antibodies under emergency authorization, navigated constantly shifting supply, adapted our delivery infrastructure repeatedly, and coordinated across institutions to measure the impact. We had mobilized something substantial. The fact that the virus eventually outran the tools doesn't diminish that.

sections.heading: The Game Had Changed
sections.text: By November 2022, the pandemic that existed when we first administered bamlanivimab was barely recognizable. The lethality of the disease had dropped dramatically — a product of vaccination, prior infection, and the evolutionary trajectory of the virus itself. Waves had come and gone, each one different from the last. The patients we were treating, and what they faced, were not the same as the patients from two years earlier. Losing the last monoclonal antibody stung, but it landed in a different context than it would have in 2021.

sections.heading: The Closing of a Chapter
sections.text: Other monoclonals would eventually come — the pipeline doesn't stop, and the need for outpatient treatment options doesn't disappear. But something ended here. The intense, two-year sprint of the monoclonal antibody era — the weekly nowcast checks, the ethical weight of supply decisions, the constant pivoting — was over. It felt like the closing of a chapter in the pandemic. Not a victory, exactly. But not a defeat either. Something more complicated: an effort that mattered, meeting an adversary that proved relentless.

personal_nodes.label: Monoclonal Antibody Outcomes in Older Adults Published
personal_nodes.content: Our retrospective study examining the impact of monoclonal antibody therapy on older adults is published in the Journal of Medical Virology. First submitted in November 2020 after rejections from several journals.
personal_nodes.preview: Our retrospective study examining the impact of monoclonal antibody therapy on older adults is published in the Journal of Medical Virology. First submitted in November 2020 after rejections from several journals.

sections.heading: It Took a Push
sections.text: I needed someone to push me on this one. After the HCQ paper and everything else that had been going on, the energy to take on another research project wasn't easy to find. It was my ED pharmacy colleague who made the case — that we had real-world data worth sharing and that we owed it to the work to document what we'd seen. She was right. I'm glad she pushed.

sections.heading: Worth Measuring
sections.text: There's something important about stopping to measure the impact of what you've done, especially in the middle of a sustained effort where the work never really pauses. We had operationalized monoclonal antibody therapy across our system under extraordinary circumstances. Asking whether it actually helped — particularly in a population like older adults who were at highest risk — was the right question to ask. The answer mattered, even if we already had a sense of it.

sections.heading: The Value of Cross-Institutional Work
sections.text: One of the things I was proudest of was the coordination across institutions. Each site had navigated the same EUAs, the same supply constraints, the same logistical challenges, but independently. Pooling that experience into a shared analysis gave us something none of us could have produced alone — a larger, more representative picture of how this therapy was actually being used and what it was doing in the real world.

sections.heading: The Limits of What We Could Say
sections.text: The paper also had real constraints, and being honest about them mattered. The pandemic was not standing still. By the time we published, the variant landscape had shifted, multiple monoclonals had come and gone, and baseline immunity had changed substantially with both vaccination and repeat infections. How much did our experience — rooted in a specific window of time, specific agents, a largely pre-vaccine population — extrapolate to what others were seeing? The honest answer was: partially, and with caution. That's still something. But it was a reminder of how quickly the ground moves under research conducted during a pandemic.

chapters.title: The Vaccine Rollout
chapters.subtitle: From 50% Threshold to 300 Shots
chapters.mood_description: A rare chapter of optimism in a dark year. The arrival of vaccines — 90% efficacy news that blew everyone away — and the shared joy of vaccination clinics. Pharmacists give hope a shot.
chapters.introduction: When the Pfizer efficacy data came in at 90%, it didn't feel real. Nothing in the prior year had prepared us for good news at that scale. I got my immunizer license — not because direct patient care was my comfort zone, but because I knew this was going to be the most important thing any of us would be part of. Over the next four months, I gave more than 300 vaccinations. It was the closest thing to joy I had felt in a year.

clinical_nodes.label: FDA Vaccine Efficacy Threshold
clinical_nodes.content: FDA publishes guidance for industry: a Covid-19 vaccine must demonstrate at least 50% efficacy to receive authorization.
clinical_nodes.preview: FDA publishes guidance for industry: a Covid-19 vaccine must demonstrate at least 50% efficacy to receive authorization.

sections.heading: The Importance of a Goalpost
sections.text: This moment stood out to me in a way I didn't fully expect. Up to this point, the path from a totally new disease to candidate therapies to an approved product had felt abstract — something that happened in regulatory documents and clinical trial pipelines I had never had reason to follow closely. What struck me about this guidance was how fundamental it was: before any of that could happen, someone had to define what success looks like. Fifty percent. That's the line. Somewhat arbitrary? Yes. But also necessary. You can't run toward a finish line that hasn't been drawn yet.

clinical_nodes.label: A Pharmacy Leader Cautions Against Vaccine
clinical_nodes.content: A prominent pharmacy administrator emails a large listserv urging extreme caution: recommends waiting for full FDA approval PLUS 6 months of post-marketing data before vaccinating. Advises pharmacists to carry liability insurance.
clinical_nodes.preview: A prominent pharmacy administrator emails a large listserv urging extreme caution: recommends waiting for full FDA approval PLUS 6 months of post-marketing data before vaccinating. Advises pharmacists to carry liability insurance.

sections.heading: Shocked
sections.text: I was genuinely shocked. This wasn't a fringe voice — this was leadership at the state level, with reach across the profession. The message completely undermined trust in the scientific process at exactly the moment we needed to be building it. If healthcare providers wouldn't advocate for the rigor of randomized controlled trials and thorough safety assessments, who would?

sections.heading: How People Handle Uncertainty
sections.text: At a broader level, it reflected something I was seeing everywhere — in debates about masking, social distancing, treatment decisions. The pandemic upended how people perceive risk. Suddenly every decision carried a new, uncertain calculus with no precedent to lean on. For some, that uncertainty produced volatile responses. And I think I began to understand why: their judgment wasn't built on first principles. It was built on experience and routine. When that scaffolding was gone, there was nothing underneath.

sections.heading: The Case for First Principles
sections.text: The pandemic drove home to me how essential it is to anchor decisions in logic and underlying principles — not habit, not comfort. Randomized controlled trials. Thorough safety assessments. These aren't bureaucratic formalities. They are how you actually learn whether something is safe and effective. The vaccines went through that process. And they worked. As healthcare providers, our job wasn't to add to the noise or the fear. It was to lead — to guide patients toward what the evidence showed, clearly and without panic.

clinical_nodes.label: FDA: Two Months Safety Data Required
clinical_nodes.content: FDA announces it requires two months of safety data before issuing any vaccine EUA, effectively eliminating the possibility of pre-election approval.

clinical_nodes.label: Vaccine Task Force Formed
clinical_nodes.content: First email from the Chief Pharmacy Officer about an internal vaccine task force formation.

clinical_nodes.label: Pfizer 90% Efficacy Announced
clinical_nodes.content: Pfizer vaccine trial reports 90% efficacy — a result that stuns experts across the field.

clinical_nodes.label: FDA VRBPAC
clinical_nodes.content: I watch most of the FDA Vaccines and Related Biological Products Advisory Committee meeting on the Pfizer vaccine. I will watch many more VRBPAC and ACIP meetings over the next year.
clinical_nodes.preview: I watch most of the FDA Vaccines and Related Biological Products Advisory Committee meeting on the Pfizer vaccine. I will watch many more VRBPAC and ACIP meetings over the next year.

sections.heading: A Companion to the NEJM Podcast
sections.text: Watching the VRBPAC was a natural extension of what I had been getting from the NEJM podcast — another window into how experts actually engage with evidence in real time. Some of the same names appeared. And like the podcast, the quality of reasoning varied. Some participants were rigorous and precise, following the data carefully and staying within what it could support. Others were less so — more impressionistic, more prone to letting adjacent considerations blur the question in front of them. Watching that contrast play out across a long meeting was itself instructive.

sections.heading: When Perception Enters the Room
sections.text: What I found most striking — and a little unsettling — was how some committee members let the optics of their decision factor into their reasoning. Not just 'what does the data show' but 'how will this recommendation be received.' That's understandable in a political context. But in a scientific advisory committee, it's a meaningful departure from the purpose of the exercise. The question on the table is whether the evidence supports authorization. How the public might interpret a yes or a no is a different question, and conflating the two muddies both.

sections.heading: Raw Data and Hard Numbers
sections.text: One of the things that made these meetings genuinely valuable was the data that came with them. The FDA presentations included the actual numbers behind the benefit-risk calculations — event rates, confidence intervals, projections for different populations and age groups. This was exactly the kind of material that's hard to communicate to the public but essential for understanding why the committee was making the calls it made. I found it far more useful than the press releases and news coverage that followed.

sections.heading: The Public Comment Period
sections.text: The open comment periods were something else entirely. Members of the public could address the committee directly, and the full range of American opinion on vaccines showed up. Scientists, advocates, skeptics, the genuinely frightened, the ideologically committed — the whole spectrum. It was at times hard to listen to, and at times genuinely moving. But it was a real and transparent part of the process, and I respected that it existed at all.

sections.heading: How a Committee Decides
sections.text: I also learned something procedural that has stayed with me: this is how you get a large group of experts to a clear, decisive outcome. A proposal is put on the table. The committee discusses it. Then they vote — yes or no. No endless iteration, no consensus by exhaustion. The structure forces resolution. It's a technique worth understanding, because the alternative — open-ended deliberation without a forcing function — tends to produce ambiguity rather than clarity.

clinical_nodes.label: Pfizer EUA Granted
clinical_nodes.content: FDA grants EUA for the Pfizer-BioNTech Covid-19 vaccine.

clinical_nodes.label: I Get My Pharmacist Immunizer License
clinical_nodes.content: I obtain my pharmacist immunizer license. Hands-on patient care is not my comfort zone — but I knew this was going to be a big deal, and I had to be a part of it.
clinical_nodes.preview: I obtain my pharmacist immunizer license. Hands-on patient care is not my comfort zone — but I knew this was going to be a big deal, and I had to be a part of it.

sections.heading: Outside My Comfort Zone, On Purpose
sections.text: Truthfully, direct patient care — the hands-on work — is not really where I live professionally. It has never been my comfort zone. But I knew what was coming. This was going to be the largest vaccination campaign in history, and it was going to matter enormously. That was enough. I didn't need it to be easy or comfortable. I needed to be part of it.

clinical_nodes.label: Moderna EUA Granted
clinical_nodes.content: FDA grants EUA for the Moderna Covid-19 vaccine.

clinical_nodes.label: First Vaccines at the Hospital
clinical_nodes.content: First day of vaccine administration at our hospital.

clinical_nodes.label: I Start Vaccinating
clinical_nodes.content: My first day administering vaccinations. Initially staff only, then the public. I provide over 300 vaccinations over the next four months at our hospital, a county vaccination site at a former mall, and an elementary school gym.

personal_nodes.label: NEJM Podcast Revelation
personal_nodes.content: I start listening to the NEJM Interviews podcast — weekly Covid-19 literature updates. My first realization that vaccine mandates might happen, that this would be the biggest vaccination campaign in history, and that I need to get licensed to vaccinate.
personal_nodes.preview: I start listening to the NEJM Interviews podcast — weekly Covid-19 literature updates. My first realization that vaccine mandates might happen, that this would be the biggest vaccination campaign in history, and that I need to get licensed to vaccinate.

sections.heading: More Than a News Update
sections.text: I came to these podcasts for the weekly content summaries — a way to stay current when the literature was moving faster than anyone could keep up with. What I got was considerably more than that. The conversations modeled something I hadn't seen done as well anywhere else: how to engage seriously with a study, extract what it actually shows, and be honest about what it doesn't. That combination turned out to have a lasting impact on how I practice, far beyond anything specific I learned about Covid.

sections.heading: Observational vs. RCT — What the Design Actually Tells You
sections.text: A lot of the early Covid literature was observational — and a lot of it was being interpreted as if it weren't. These podcasts were rigorous about that distinction without being dismissive. An observational study can tell you something real. It can generate a hypothesis, suggest a signal, give you a sense of magnitude. What it can't do is establish causation the way a randomized controlled trial can. Understanding where that line is — and why it exists — changed how I read everything that came across my desk. Not every study needs to be an RCT to be useful. But you have to know what you're holding.

sections.heading: Communicating What We Know and Don't
sections.text: The other thing that stayed with me was how the hosts communicated uncertainty. Not as a hedge or a disclaimer, but as a precise act. They would isolate exactly what a study could support: not 'this drug works,' not 'this drug doesn't work,' but something more specific — 'in this population, under these conditions, with this endpoint, here is what the data show, and here is where the inference ends.' That kind of calibration is genuinely hard. It requires you to hold confidence and humility at the same time. It became a model for how I tried to communicate with the clinicians I worked with throughout the pandemic.

embed.label: Watch the NEJM Covid-19 Interviews Playlist on YouTube

personal_nodes.label: When Leadership Gets It Wrong
personal_nodes.content: A state pharmacy leader urges colleagues to avoid the vaccine. I was shocked — and troubled by what it revealed about how people reason under uncertainty.
personal_nodes.preview: A state pharmacy leader urges colleagues to avoid the vaccine. I was shocked — and troubled by what it revealed about how people reason under uncertainty.

sections.heading: Reasoning From First Principles
sections.text: What troubled me most wasn't the conclusion — it was the reasoning behind it. Someone with a long career in pharmacy, a position of leadership, and presumably a deep knowledge base had looked at this situation and arrived at a genuinely harmful recommendation. That doesn't happen by accident. It happens when someone applies pattern-matching from past experience to a situation that doesn't fit the pattern — and doesn't notice the mismatch. Reasoning from first principles means going back to the underlying logic: what do we know about vaccine development, about trial design, about how regulatory authorization works, about the disease itself? That kind of reasoning is slower and less comfortable than drawing on experience. But it's what you need when the situation is actually new.

sections.heading: Experience Without Foundation
sections.text: Experience is genuinely valuable. It builds intuition, pattern recognition, and the ability to act quickly in familiar territory. But it has a critical vulnerability: it only works when the new situation resembles the ones that built it. Drop someone into a truly novel environment — a new pathogen, a new treatment paradigm, a regulatory process moving at unprecedented speed — and experience alone gives them nothing to hold onto. What they fall back on instead is the shape of past situations, whether or not that shape applies. The leaders who navigated the pandemic well weren't necessarily the most experienced ones. They were the ones who could distinguish between what they knew from prior situations and what they actually knew about this one.

sections.heading: Disappointment in Leadership
sections.text: To be fair to experience: in stable environments, it is often enough. Someone who has spent decades in a field where the ground doesn't shift dramatically can get very far on accumulated pattern recognition. That's not a character flaw — it's a reasonable adaptation to how most professional environments actually work. What I couldn't reconcile was something different: a leader, in a moment that called for steadiness, instead showing an apparent distrust in the regulatory process and pushing messaging that edged toward fear. The public needed people in positions of authority to model how to reason carefully under uncertainty. This did the opposite. That's what disappointed me — not the limits of experience, but the failure of responsibility that came with it.

personal_nodes.label: 90% — A Pivot in the Pandemic
personal_nodes.content: The Pfizer efficacy data lands at 90%. It is thrilling — a genuine turning point. I had been checking Twitter constantly all year and somehow a resident beat me to it.
personal_nodes.preview: The Pfizer efficacy data lands at 90%. It is thrilling — a genuine turning point. I had been checking Twitter constantly all year and somehow a resident beat me to it.

sections.heading: How I Found Out
sections.text: I had been checking Twitter two to four times a day for most of the year — it had become one of the primary ways breaking medical news traveled fastest. So when Pfizer announced 90% efficacy, the fact that a resident told me before I saw it myself was genuinely surprising. A small thing, but it stuck. After months of monitoring, I missed the biggest piece of news the year had to offer.

sections.heading: What the Data Actually Showed
sections.text: The headline number was stunning — 90% efficacy far exceeded what most people in the field had expected or hoped for. But it's worth being precise about what the trial was designed to show. The primary endpoint was symptomatic Covid-19. That's meaningful and important. But the study wasn't powered to detect an effect on severity of disease — hospitalizations, serious illness, death. And yet that's where the vaccines ultimately proved most consequential. The benefit in preventing severe outcomes turned out to be enormous, arguably the most important thing the vaccines did. The trial didn't show us that; we learned it later.

sections.heading: The Transmission Question
sections.text: Early enthusiasm — including some of mine — leaned into the idea that high vaccine efficacy against symptomatic disease would translate to reduced transmission and meaningful population-level control. That didn't fully hold up. Vaccination reduced individual risk substantially, but the vaccines' effect on onward transmission was more limited than early optimism suggested, and it waned further as variants emerged. It's not that the vaccines underperformed — they performed extraordinarily well at what they were built to do. But the story we told ourselves about where they would take us needed revision.

personal_nodes.label: My Turn: Vaccinated at 5pm
personal_nodes.content: I'm vaccinated at 5pm — an hour after the clinic is scheduled to close — as the vaccine team looks for volunteers to use up remaining doses. I was not in the first priority group.
personal_nodes.preview: I'm vaccinated at 5pm — an hour after the clinic is scheduled to close — as the vaccine team looks for volunteers to use up remaining doses. I was not in the first priority group.

sections.heading: Why I Wasn't First
sections.text: Priority groups were based on risk to staff — those with direct patient care on the COVID floors came first. Since I wasn't regularly on those floors, I wasn't in that initial group. But the vaccine vials had a short expiration window once opened, and the clinic team wasn't about to let doses go to waste. They went out into the halls and offices looking for anyone willing. Someone walked into a meeting I was in. I didn't hesitate.

sections.heading: Words Only Go So Far
sections.text: With so much public uncertainty about the vaccine — and so much riding on getting people vaccinated — I knew that advocacy only carries so much weight. Words only go so far. I knew how important it would be, in every future conversation I had about the vaccine, to be able to say: the moment I had the opportunity, I took it. No deliberating. No waiting. That moment, I think, was worth more than anything I could have written or presented.

personal_nodes.label: Joy at the Vaccine Clinic
personal_nodes.content: 'I remember the shared sense of positivity, excitement and joy among the vaccinators and public. Everyone was happier than they had been in nearly a year.'
personal_nodes.preview: 'I remember the shared sense of positivity, excitement and joy among the vaccinators and public. Everyone was happier than they had been in nearly a year.'

sections.heading: A Small Part of Something Very Big
sections.text: My role was not complicated. I wasn't doing anything terribly difficult. But I was a small part of something very big — and that distinction mattered more than I expected. There was real satisfaction in it: comforting the people who were nervous, sharing in the excitement of those who were eager. After a year of so much uncertainty and heaviness, the clinic felt like a release valve.

sections.heading: A Few Notable Arms
sections.text: Among the people I vaccinated: the Chair of Medicine, several physicians I worked closely with, an NHL player, and some minor league players. I also supervised technicians vaccinating a first-round NHL draft pick. In a year defined by so much loss and uncertainty, there was something quietly meaningful about being on the giving end of this.

personal_nodes.label: The CPO Calls Before a TV Appearance
personal_nodes.content: The Chief Pharmacy Officer calls me 20 minutes before a television appearance asking how to explain how the vaccines work.

chapters.title: Fox and Friends
chapters.subtitle: The HCQ Conflict Comes to a Head
chapters.mood_description: A concentrated drama centered on Dr. Smith's continued advocacy for hydroxychloroquine after the evidence had turned against it — culminating in a Fox News appearance where I am named by Dr. Smith, and the COO privately drops f-bombs in my defense.
chapters.introduction: This story runs parallel to much of what came before. It traces a conflict that threaded through the entire pandemic — Dr. Smith's continued advocacy for hydroxychloroquine long after the evidence had turned against it. It's a story about what happens when ego meets evidence, and about what it felt like to be named on national television by someone I disagreed with.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ Dosing Conflict
clinical_nodes.preview: Dr. Smith emails hospital leadership demanding higher HCQ dosing based on a non-comparative French study. A heated email exchange and phone call follow.
clinical_nodes.content: Dr. Smith emails the CEO, CMO, and Chair of Medicine demanding higher HCQ dosing based on a non-comparative French follow-up study. A heated exchange follows.

sections.heading: The Demand
sections.text: Smith emails the CEO, CMO, and Chair of Medicine pushing for higher HCQ dosing based on a retrospective, non-comparative French follow-up study. He makes strong claims about the data — 'Since patients on this regimen will clear the virus very quickly…' — raises supply concerns about the current regimen, and implies he might be blocked from using the French dosing. He wasn't.

sections.heading: My Response
sections.text: I respond by email, emphasizing the major limitations: the study is non-comparative, only 15% of patients were on oxygen, and significant questions remain about the original study. A loading dose with shorter duration makes pharmacokinetic sense. Better evidence supports our regimen, though all evidence remains limited. Supply is a real concern. I commit to advocating for a shorter duration and close with gratitude for everyone's work.

sections.heading: 'Stabbed Him in the Back'
sections.text: Smith calls immediately after reading my response. He tells me I stabbed him in the back. As with most conversations with him, the tension gives way quickly — he drifts into other topics and the call ends without further conflict.

clinical_nodes.label: FDA Emergency Use Authorization
clinical_nodes.content: FDA issues EUA for chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine.

clinical_nodes.label: Dr. Smith on Fox News
clinical_nodes.content: ID physician Dr. Stephen Smith appears on Fox & Friends stating hydroxychloroquine is "the beginning of the end of the pandemic."
clinical_nodes.preview: ID physician Dr. Stephen Smith appears on Fox & Friends stating hydroxychloroquine is "the beginning of the end of the pandemic."

sections.heading: How Does a Physician End Up on Fox & Friends?
sections.text: The shock of seeing this was twofold. First: that he was on national television at all. Fox & Friends reaches millions of viewers. This wasn't a local interview or a hospital press release — it was one of the most-watched morning programs in the country. Getting there requires connections, coordination, and a willingness to step into a political media ecosystem that most clinicians stay far away from. That Dr. Smith, affiliated with our hospital system, was sitting in that chair was, by itself, stunning.

sections.heading: The Claim Itself
sections.text: Then there was what he actually said. 'The beginning of the end of the pandemic.' Not 'this looks promising' or 'early data is encouraging' — the kind of hedged language any scientist would reach for when the evidence is still thin. A definitive, sweeping declaration that a drug we were still figuring out had effectively solved the crisis. The pandemic had been going for weeks. Thousands were dying. And Dr. Smith, with a national platform, was telling the country it was essentially over. That was a stunning thing to say out loud.

embed.label: Watch the Segment on Fox News

clinical_nodes.label: Oval Office Meeting
clinical_nodes.content: The day after his Fox News appearance, Dr. Smith meets with the President, a TV host, and the FDA Director. HCQ had already received emergency authorization — at least part of the meeting was apparently spent pushing to authorize higher dosing.
clinical_nodes.preview: The day after his Fox News appearance, Dr. Smith meets with the President, a TV host, and the FDA Director. HCQ had already received emergency authorization — at least part of the meeting was apparently spent pushing to authorize higher dosing.

sections.heading: Already Authorized — He Wanted More
sections.text: HCQ had already received an FDA Emergency Use Authorization five days earlier, on March 28th. The meeting wasn't about getting HCQ approved — that had already happened. Based on what was later documented, at least part of the conversation centered on pushing for authorization of higher dosing regimens. The EUA as written had limitations. He wanted to expand them.

sections.heading: I Didn't Know
sections.text: I was completely unaware this meeting had happened. It wasn't something I learned about in the moment — or for a long time afterward. I only found out when I stumbled across the House Oversight Committee's report to Congress, published in August 2022, which documented the Trump White House's efforts to pressure the FDA's coronavirus response. The meeting was in there. It was a strange thing to read: an event from early in the pandemic, involving someone I had been dealing with directly, that I had never known about at the time.

sections.heading: How I Think He Got There
sections.text: I can't say for certain how Dr. Smith ended up in the Oval Office. But the sequence is suggestive. He appeared on Fox & Friends on April 2nd calling hydroxychloroquine 'the beginning of the end of the pandemic.' The President was a regular Fox viewer and had already been promoting HCQ publicly. My best guess is that Trump saw the segment, heard what he wanted to hear, and the invitation followed from there. Whether or not that's exactly what happened, it's the most plausible path from a cable news appearance to a meeting with the FDA Director.

clinical_nodes.label: P&T Memo: HCQ Prohibited
clinical_nodes.content: I work with the CMO, the Pharmacy Director, and the Associate Director on a P&T memo to stop HCQ use for Covid-19. The ID division votes on prohibition — passes by a narrow margin. The ID co-chair is completely unaware of the process; he does not check email.

clinical_nodes.label: CMO vs. ID Physician Over HCQ Ban
clinical_nodes.content: The CMO and Dr. Smith clash directly over the HCQ ban.

clinical_nodes.label: Fox News Appearance — My Name Used
clinical_nodes.content: Dr. Smith returns to Fox News claiming his unpublished, retrospective study proves hydroxychloroquine works. He names me by name on national television: 'I turned to the pharmacist, Steve Smoke at St. Barnabas and I said, I got people that are literally more than four times difference in weight, one or both groups is getting the wrong dose.' The study is vastly inferior to available evidence and — to my knowledge — was never published.
clinical_nodes.preview: Dr. Smith returns to Fox News claiming his unpublished study proves HCQ works — and names me by name. By 2021, the science on HCQ had been settled for a long time. The shock wasn't just that he was back. It was that he was still pushing this.

sections.heading: The Silence Before
sections.text: I hadn't spoken to Dr. Smith about HCQ in some time. There came a point where it felt like everything that needed to be said had been said — the evidence had spoken, our guidelines had changed, and continuing to argue felt beside the point. What I didn't expect was for him to resurface on national television as if none of that had happened.

sections.heading: The Science Was Long Settled
sections.text: By June 2021, HCQ for Covid-19 wasn't a point of controversy within ID — and hadn't been for a long time. The RECOVERY trial, the revoked EUA, study after study pointing the same direction. Dr. Smith's study was retrospective, unpublished, and vastly inferior in quality to what was already in the literature. The shock wasn't just that he was on Fox News again. It was that he was still pushing this, a year and a half in, as if the conversation hadn't moved on.

sections.heading: Hearing My Name
sections.text: What was genuinely strange was hearing about it from others — family members, coworkers reaching out surprised, a little amused. But when I thought about it: my name was pretty irrelevant to the actual story. I doubt he even realized he'd said it. He wasn't trying to name me — he was bashing the hospital. That was not new for him.

sections.heading: The Scale Was Wild
sections.text: What was new was the scale. Criticizing hospital decisions internally is one thing. Doing it on a national television network, to millions of viewers, while citing an unpublished study in support of a treatment the scientific community had conclusively rejected — that was something else entirely.

embed.label: Watch the Segment on Fox News

clinical_nodes.label: Vindication: UTI Pathway Praised
clinical_nodes.content: The Chair of Medicine sends an email praising my work on an unrelated UTI antimicrobial stewardship pathway to the CEO, CFO, and CMO. The CMO responds in agreement.

clinical_nodes.label: RECOVERY Trial: HCQ No Benefit
clinical_nodes.content: The RECOVERY trial determines no benefit from hydroxychloroquine in hospitalized Covid-19 patients. I distribute the details and clinical trial contact info to ID physicians.

clinical_nodes.label: HCQ EUA Revoked
clinical_nodes.content: FDA officially revokes the emergency use authorization for hydroxychloroquine.

clinical_nodes.label: RECOVERY Trial: Dexamethasone Reduces Mortality
clinical_nodes.content: The RECOVERY trial reports dexamethasone reduces mortality — a landmark finding. Questions arise about whether a trial report (not peer-reviewed) is sufficient to change practice. Our system waits for the NIH guideline update but the CPO is pressured by an ID physician to act sooner.

embed.label: Read the RECOVERY Trial Dexamethasone Results

clinical_nodes.label: Ivermectin Returns — Physician Confrontation
clinical_nodes.content: Two ID physicians order ivermectin. I push back. One is very rude: 'How dare you?… I've been practicing medicine for X years…' The physician compares the situation to bamlanivimab. Bamlanivimab is later proven effective; ivermectin is not.

personal_nodes.label: COO Drops F-Bombs
personal_nodes.content: The hospital COO visits my office privately to offer personal support and express frustration with Dr. Smith.

personal_nodes.label: HCQ Paper Accepted
personal_nodes.content: My hydroxychloroquine paper is accepted for publication (submitted in February 2021, after rejection from several journals).

personal_nodes.label: My Expertise Stops Mattering
personal_nodes.content: As politics overtook the pandemic, the friends and family who had sought my expertise stopped valuing it. Walking in and out of the hospital every day while the people closest to me grew skeptical.
personal_nodes.preview: As politics overtook the pandemic, the friends and family who had sought my expertise stopped valuing it. Walking in and out of the hospital every day while the people closest to me grew skeptical.

sections.heading: A Strange Betrayal
sections.text: Early in the pandemic, they called, texted, asked what I was seeing, what they should do. But as politics overtook the pandemic, that changed. My firsthand experience and years of expertise weren't needed anymore — they had other sources of information. The same people who had sought my opinion weeks earlier were now skeptical of what I was telling them. It was a strange and personal betrayal, losing credibility with the people closest to you while walking in and out of the hospital every day.

personal_nodes.label: Opinion Article Published
personal_nodes.content: My opinion article published in Pharmacy Practice News — five months in, reflecting on what the pandemic has taught me about expertise, uncertainty, and heroism.
personal_nodes.preview: My opinion article published in Pharmacy Practice News — five months in, reflecting on what the pandemic has taught me about expertise, uncertainty, and heroism.

sections.heading: What the Pandemic Taught Me
sections.text: My most common answer has been the most difficult one — 'I don't know.' Sharing speculation based on flimsy evidence does not bring clarity. Knowing when a question isn't yours to answer is just as important as answering it. And buried in the devastation are a million stories of heroism. Ask for those stories. Tell those stories. And listen to them.
